summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the parallel evolution of personal computers and his own life, sparked by revisiting Robert X. Cringely's 'Accidental Empires' book and its PBS documentary adaptation 'Triumph of the Nerds.' He traces the rapid transformation from the primitive Altair 8800 in 1975 to the archetypal Apple II just two years later, noting how Apple established the template for all modern personal computers. Drawing from his own experience growing up with Apple computers and learning AppleBASIC, Atwood concludes that despite 30+ years of progress, both computers and he himself still have significant maturing to do.
key_points: The Apple II (1977) established the template for all modern personal computers: plastic case, expansion slots, CRT display, integrated keyboard, and floppy disk drive
The transformation from Altair 8800 to Apple II in just two years represents a remarkable leap in usability and design
VisiCalc was the first killer app that drove personal computer adoption before the IBM PC era
The author's formative programming experiences in AppleBASIC shaped his career despite never transitioning to the more expensive Macintosh
money_quotes: I've always felt like the home computer and I grew up together.
The Altair is barely recognizable as a personal computer, even though it is technically the first one.
Every modern home computer released after 1977 followed the template that Apple established for the industry.
It's difficult to get excited about a machine with no display and a row of dip-switches for input.
Computers still have a long way to go before they're fully grown up. And so do I.
key_insight: The rapid evolution from primitive hardware to usable personal computers in the late 1970s established patterns we still follow today, yet both technology and those who grew up with it remain works in progress.
